posts.title: Humpback Blackdevil Anglerfish
posts.text:
Melanocetus johnsonii. The humpback blackdevil. About six inches long, all mouth, with a bioluminescent lure dangling from its forehead.

It lives between 200 and 4,000 meters. Complete darkness. The lure is the only light source for hundreds of meters in any direction. Small fish swim toward it. That's the whole strategy.

The males are tiny — less than an inch. When a male finds a female, he bites into her skin and never lets go. His body fuses with hers. His eyes degenerate. His organs dissolve. He becomes a permanent sperm-producing parasite attached to her side. She can carry six males at once.

Photo: Christopher Gurr, Bermuda Aquarium. CC BY-NC.

Photo: Christopher Gurr. CC BY-NC.

posts.title: Atolla Jelly - Deep Sea Crown Jellyfish
posts.text:
Atolla wyvillei. The alarm jelly. Pulled from 290 meters in the Labrador Sea, caught in a shrimp trawl net.

When something attacks an Atolla, it doesn't swim away. It can't. Instead it lights up — a rotating ring of bioluminescent flashes that spin around its bell like a siren. The display is called a "burglar alarm." The logic: attract something bigger than whatever's eating you. Let the bigger thing eat your attacker.

It works often enough that the strategy survived millions of years of evolution.

The Atolla lives in every ocean on earth. One of the most common deep-sea jellies in existence. Almost nobody's ever seen one alive. They disintegrate in minutes at surface pressure.

Photo: Jean-Francois Rousseau, Labrador Sea, Feb 2026. CC BY-NC.

tweets.text:

Works often enough that the strategy survived millions of years.

Lives in every ocean on earth. One of the most common deep-sea jellies. Almost nobody's seen one alive. They disintegrate at surface pressure.

Photo: Jean-Francois Rousseau. CC BY-NC.

posts.title: Giant Tube Worm from Hydrothermal Vent
posts.text:
Riftia pachyptila. Giant tube worm. Collected at 2,520 meters depth from a hydrothermal vent on the East Pacific Rise.

The Mir-2 submersible brought this one up during the 49th cruise of the R/V Akademik Mstislav Keldysh in 2003. Same ship and submersible that filmed the Titanic wreckage.

These worms have no mouth, no stomach, no gut. Nothing. They feed entirely through bacteria living inside a specialized organ called a trophosome. The bacteria convert hydrogen sulfide from the vent water into energy. The worm provides housing. The bacteria provide food.

They grow up to 2.4 meters long. Growth rate: 85 centimeters per year in good conditions. That's the fastest of any known deep-sea invertebrate.

The water around them is 2°C. Twenty centimeters away, at the vent opening, it's 400°C.

Photo: Georgy Vinogradov, R/V Keldysh, 2003. CC BY.

posts.title: Vampire Squid
posts.text:
Vampyroteuthis infernalis. Literally: "vampire squid from hell." Found at about 2,500 feet depth.

It's neither squid nor octopus. It's the only surviving member of its own order, Vampyromorphida. A living fossil. Its closest relatives have been dead for 300 million years.

The "vampire" name comes from the webbing between its arms, which it can invert over its body like a cloak. The red eyes are misleading — they're actually the largest eyes-to-body ratio of any animal on earth.

It doesn't hunt. It eats marine snow — dead organic matter that drifts down from the sunlit zone above. Detritus. Corpse confetti. It catches it with two retractable filaments that trail behind it like fishing lines.

Maximum speed: two body lengths per second. It's not in a hurry. Nothing at 900 meters is.

Photo: CC BY-NC.

posts.title: Giant Isopod
posts.text:
Bathynomus giganteus. Giant isopod. Basically a pill bug that grew to the size of a football.

They live between 170 and 2,140 meters on the ocean floor. Scavengers. They eat whatever falls from above — dead whales, fish carcasses, squid. When food hits the bottom, isopods arrive in swarms.

They can go five years without eating. Five. Years. Their metabolism is so slow that they've been observed sitting completely motionless on the seafloor for weeks. Researchers aren't sure if that counts as sleeping.

Seven pairs of legs. Compound eyes with about 3,500 facets each — unusually good vision for something that lives in near-total darkness. Nobody's sure why they need it.

The largest specimen ever recorded was 76 centimeters. About 2.5 feet of armored crustacean.

Photo: Holly, Monterey Bay Aquarium. CC BY-NC.

posts.title: Giant Squid
posts.text:
Architeuthis dux. Giant squid. This one was found off Tenerife, Canary Islands, October 2023.

For most of human history, nobody had seen one alive. The first live giant squid wasn't filmed until 2004, by a Japanese team using a baited camera at 900 meters in the North Pacific. Before that, everything we knew came from dead ones that washed ashore or turned up in sperm whale stomachs.

They have the largest eyes of any living animal. Each one the size of a dinner plate — about 27 centimeters across. The eyes evolved to detect the bioluminescent disturbance caused by a sperm whale approaching in darkness.

Maximum recorded length: 13 meters including tentacles. But most of that is tentacle. The body itself is surprisingly small for something so legendary.

They live between 300 and 1,000 meters. We still don't know how many there are.

Photo: Marc Martin Sola, Tenerife. CC BY-NC.

posts.title: Deepwater Coral
posts.text:
Desmophyllum pertusum. Cold-water coral. This colony was found off Tromso, Norway at depth.

Most people think coral needs warm water and sunlight. This one builds reefs in total darkness at temperatures between 4 and 12°C. No photosynthesis. It feeds by filtering particles from the current.

Norwegian cold-water coral reefs are some of the largest biological structures in the North Atlantic. The Rost Reef off Lofoten stretches 40 kilometers. It wasn't discovered until 2002.

These reefs grow about 25 millimeters per year. Some of the structures are 8,000 years old. They support over 1,300 species — more than some tropical reefs.

Bottom trawling has destroyed an estimated 30-50% of Norway's cold-water coral. The government banned trawling over known reefs in 1999. Finding them all is the hard part.

Photo: Jakob Tougaard, Tromso. CC BY.
